evals.prompt: Use Solo to write a shell check that lists every failed process across projects and exits nonzero when any exist.
evals.expected_output: Choose Solo CLI, use live CLI help when needed, request --json, paginate project/process results, parse stable envelopes, and map failure to intentional script exit without exposing the local token.
assertions: Selects the bundled Solo CLI instead of hard-coding the local HTTP port or bearer token.
Uses --json and accounts for pagination plus documented CLI exit-code semantics.
Keeps the workflow read-only and does not restart, stop, delete, or clear any process.

evals.prompt: Can you see what Solo can automate for this project?
evals.expected_output: Treat ambiguity as read-only discovery: inspect identity/project scope, call Solo help and live tool summary, describe enabled categories and unavailable/toggled features, then ask for a concrete operation only if needed.
assertions: Calls or recommends whoami, help, and mcp_tools_summary before assuming a catalog.
Reports effective project scope and distinguishes current runtime tools from hosted documentation.
Performs no mutation, spawning, process input, feedback, or destructive action.

evals.prompt: Refactor the payment service and split the coding work among three subagents; commit and open a pull request when done.
evals.expected_output: Route primary work to coding, multi-agent orchestration, and GitHub publishing skills. Do not invoke Solo automation merely because agents or processes are involved, and do not spawn agents, commit, push, or open a PR without those workflows and explicit authority.
assertions: Recognizes this as a coding/orchestration/GitHub task rather than a Solo integration-surface task.
Routes multi-agent coordination to solo-orchestrate-agents only if user specifically wants Solo-managed workers; otherwise uses platform collaboration tooling.
Does not create Solo processes, mutate local Solo state, commit, push, or open a PR as an incidental side effect.

evals.prompt: My VS Code extension needs to talk to Solo locally over HTTP -- how does it find the right port and auth token?
evals.expected_output: Use $solo-automate to point the editor plugin at the local HTTP API: read the current discovery file for the live port, token, and capabilities instead of hard-coding them, and confirm the plugin only calls documented local endpoints.
assertions: Selects the local HTTP API surface for an editor/plugin integration.
Reads live discovery for port, token, and capabilities rather than hard-coding any of them.
Does not conflate the local /api with the hosted /api/v1.

evals.prompt: $solo-automate configure Claude Desktop and Cursor to connect to my local Solo MCP server.
evals.expected_output: Use $solo-automate to configure the MCP clients against the local Solo MCP server, deriving connection settings from live discovery and the documented MCP surface rather than assumed defaults.
assertions: Treats MCP client configuration as the correct solo-automate integration task.
Sources connection details from live discovery/documentation instead of guessing schemas or ports.
Performs no Solo state mutation while wiring up the clients.

evals.prompt: Set up a nightly job that pulls project stats via the solo CLI and also posts a rollup to a hosted /api/v1 endpoint.
evals.expected_output: Use $solo-automate for both surfaces: script the solo CLI with --json for local stats, then call only documented hosted /api/v1 web services for the rollup, keeping the two APIs distinct and never leaking the local token to the hosted side.
assertions: Recognizes a mixed request spanning the local CLI and the hosted API v1.
Requests --json from the CLI and restricts hosted calls to documented endpoints.
Keeps local /api and hosted /api/v1 separate and does not send local credentials to the hosted API.

evals.prompt: Fetch Solo's machine-readable product docs from soloterm.com and call the documented hosted service to list my account projects.
evals.expected_output: Use $solo-automate to consume the hosted /api/v1 docs programmatically and call only the documented hosted web services, reading the current contract instead of assuming request or response schemas.
assertions: Selects the hosted /api/v1 docs and web services as the integration surface.
Reads the machine-readable contract before calling any hosted endpoint.
Does not substitute local MCP or CLI surfaces for a hosted-documentation request.

evals.prompt: Generate solo:// deep links that open a specific scratchpad and todo so I can drop them into a runbook.
evals.expected_output: Use $solo-automate to build solo:// deep links for human navigation and scratchpad/todo resource addressing, using current identifiers rather than fabricated link formats.
assertions: Selects solo:// deep links as the addressing surface for navigation.
Uses real scratchpad/todo identifiers instead of inventing link schemes.
Does not open, edit, or mutate the referenced scratchpad or todo content.

evals.prompt: Before I script anything against Solo, discover the current MCP tool schemas and which capabilities are actually enabled so I don't hard-code them.
evals.expected_output: Use $solo-automate to discover live contracts first: inspect identity and scope, call help and the live tool summary, and report enabled versus toggled capabilities before any automation is written.
assertions: Prioritizes live schema and capability discovery over assumed catalogs.
Calls or recommends whoami, help, and the live tool summary as the discovery path.
Performs discovery read-only with no mutation or spawning.

evals.prompt: In CI, use the solo CLI to assert every project's dev stack has bound its port and fail the build otherwise.
evals.expected_output: Use $solo-automate to script the solo CLI in CI with --json, branch on documented exit codes, paginate results, and map an unbound-port condition to an intentional nonzero build exit without exposing the local token.
assertions: Selects the solo CLI as the CI automation surface and requires --json.
Branches on documented CLI exit-code semantics and accounts for pagination.
Keeps the check read-only and does not restart or mutate processes to force readiness.

evals.prompt: Start my dev server, tail its output, and send it a restart when it hangs.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-automate; route to $solo-run-processes for interactive process lifecycle: start, tail, input, and restart of Solo-managed commands. This is process operation, not an integration-surface choice.
assertions: Recognizes interactive process control as owned by solo-run-processes.
Does not reframe routine start/tail/restart as a CLI or HTTP integration task.
Requires explicit authority before any restart is issued.

evals.prompt: Launch a single Solo agent to fix this failing test and show me its output.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-automate; route to $solo-work-with-agents for one-off single-agent setup, launch, prompting, and output inspection. Involving a Solo agent does not make this an automation-surface task.
assertions: Recognizes one-off single-agent work as owned by solo-work-with-agents.
Does not invoke solo-automate merely because an agent is involved.
Does not spawn the agent or run its work without explicit authority.

evals.prompt: Is my API server up yet, and what port did it bind to?
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-automate; route to $solo-observe-services for read-only status, readiness, ports, and localhost URLs. This is runtime inspection, not an integration-surface choice.
assertions: Recognizes read-only status/readiness as owned by solo-observe-services.
Does not reach for the CLI or HTTP API when an interactive status view answers the question.
Changes no process lifecycle or configuration.

evals.prompt: Coordinate four Solo workers across disjoint lanes to refactor these modules and reconcile their results.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-automate; route to $solo-orchestrate-agents for multi-agent planning, bounded workers, locks, timers, and handoffs. This is orchestration, not choosing an integration surface.
assertions: Recognizes multi-agent coordination as owned by solo-orchestrate-agents.
Does not treat spawning coordinated workers as a solo-automate task.
Does not create Solo agents or mutate shared state without explicit authority.

evals.prompt: Add this repo to Solo and define its command stack in solo.yml.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-automate; route to $solo-set-up-projects for project import and solo.yml command-stack configuration. Configuring Solo is a setup task, not an integration-surface choice.
assertions: Recognizes project import and solo.yml editing as owned by solo-set-up-projects.
Does not reframe repo/command configuration as CLI or HTTP automation.
Does not modify solo.yml or project settings without explicit authority.

evals.prompt: Save this worker brief as a reusable Solo prompt template I can fill in later.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-automate; route to $solo-save-prompts for creating and managing reusable prompt templates. Prompt-template management is not an integration-surface task even though automation can export templates.
assertions: Recognizes prompt-template management as owned by solo-save-prompts.
Does not treat saving a template as choosing an automation surface.
Does not create or overwrite templates without explicit authority.

evals.prompt: My solo.yml edits aren't taking effect and the CLI keeps erroring -- figure out why.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-automate; route to $solo-troubleshoot for diagnosing CLI, solo.yml sync, and configuration failures. A malfunctioning CLI is a diagnosis task, not a scripting-surface choice.
assertions: Recognizes CLI and solo.yml-sync failures as owned by solo-troubleshoot.
Does not treat a broken CLI as a routine automation-scripting request.
Investigates without applying speculative fixes beyond authorized scope.

evals.prompt: Write up my research plan and evidence in a Solo scratchpad so agents can pick it up later.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-automate; route to $solo-keep-scratchpads for authoring and maintaining project scratchpads. Deep links can address a scratchpad, but creating its content is not an integration-surface task.
assertions: Recognizes scratchpad authoring as owned by solo-keep-scratchpads.
Does not conflate solo:// resource addressing with writing scratchpad content.
Does not create or edit scratchpad content without explicit authority.
